data.title: Bitwise Advisor: From the K-shaped economy to AI taking away jobs, how can BTC save young people?
data.description:
Source: "When Shift Happens" podcast; Compiled by: Felix, PANews

Jeff Park, a Bitwise advisor and macro strategist, was formerly the Chief Investment Officer of ProCap Financial. Recently, in a podcast, he analyzed why real estate is actually a depreciating asset, why Bitcoin is the ultimate safe haven, and how AI will trigger a wave of Bitcoin adoption. Jeff Park believes that for young people, from unaffordable housing to AI replacing an entire generation's jobs, the financial system has completely collapsed.

PANews has compiled the highlights of the conversation.

Host: You told me before that you were exposed to the concept of "currency devaluation" very early on.

... Could you elaborate on this? Jeff Park: Absolutely. I grew up in both the US and South Korea. I spent elementary school in South Korea, right in the middle of the 1997 Asian financial crisis. That crisis left a deep impression on me, even though I was only in second or third grade at the time, because I witnessed the absolute unity a nation could display when it couldn't control its own destiny. It's a strange feeling when you find people everywhere, from upstairs to downstairs, bound together by a patriotism directly linked to the value of their sovereign currency. Perhaps the most relatable similar event for Americans is 9/11, which united people from all walks of life, regardless of their political leanings, to reflect on "what America is." In South Korea, the widespread devaluation of the currency also served this purpose of national unity. I clearly remember the South Korean government calling on citizens to donate gold to restore the national treasury and repay the IMF (International Monetary Fund) bailout loans, because those loans had extremely harsh terms. In emerging markets like South Korea, the IMF is perceived as having a strong political dimension, which may have contributed to my involvement in the crypto space 20 years later. Host: You just mentioned that the currency devaluation you experienced when you were young brought people together. But that was in Asia, now it's in the United States. What's happening in the US today? Can people really come together? Jeff Park: I think America's greatest strength is also its greatest weakness, which is its diversity. Commentators in Asia often predict that "demographic diversity will destroy America." In South Korea, achieving national cohesion is easy because everyone is Korean, bound together by a shared history of resisting colonial oppression. But in the US, it's hard to find an obvious bond that inspires a spirit of sacrifice throughout society. Sacrifice is the key word. For example, in South Korea, all men, regardless of class or education, are required to serve in the military, creating homogenized social norms among men. But in the US, it's hard to find a typical "American experience" that brings people together. Politics often draws lines between left and right, upper and lower classes, young and old, but I think these are just distractions. What's truly lacking and what needs to be cherished is a sense of national identity and solidarity among the younger generation. Host: You entered the workforce just as the 2008 financial crisis hit, followed by years of accelerated money printing. Now we live in New York, the world's financial center, where everything is ridiculously expensive. I'm Swiss, now living in Singapore, and I'm used to high prices, but it's still unbelievable here. How do ordinary people survive? People have clearly felt the inflation these past few years; what exactly happened? Jeff Park: Yes, what we're seeing is a completely out-of-control, utterly collapsed financial system. The lower classes are experiencing a "K-shaped economy." A "K-shaped economy" means that one group is enjoying the economic prosperity brought about by asset inflation, while another group is experiencing recession. They can't find jobs, and the wealth gap is widening, hence the K-shape—a dual-track economy. In New York, you can clearly see this through the asset class of real estate. Over the past 10 years, the average home price in New York City hasn't actually increased; it's remained flat. Those "super mansions" that serve as stores of value are selling extremely well. They're not for living in; they're assets that the wealthy buy to preserve value on their balance sheets. If you bought a $20 million penthouse seven years ago, you might sell it for $30 million now. But if you bought a regular home intended for a real life, to support your family, and to make a real economic contribution to the city, the price has actually decreased or remained flat. For example, New York has a "mansion tax" levied on properties over $1 million. Decades ago, $1 million could indeed buy a mansion, but now in New York, $1 million only buys a studio apartment. The government deliberately doesn't link this tax to inflation so it can collect more tax revenue. New York is a paradox, and all of this is a symptom of a lack of quality assets that preserve value.

Host: Why has the real estate market become like this?

Jeff Park: Land is inherently scarce.

The United States enjoys absolute privilege in operating the global financial system, and the dollar is its largest export product. However, this comes at a cost; offshore funds must ultimately flow back and be invested in US assets to maintain the trade deficit. This creates an artificial, bubble-like market for US assets, where overseas investors simply need a place to park their money. This raises a serious problem: the pricing motives of this market have nothing to do with those of us who actually live in New York and want to settle down. Host: For someone aged 30 to 35 who has saved some money, how should they invest? I think $1 million is a lot of money, but in New York, it can only buy an apartment, while you're telling me that you have to buy a $20 million penthouse to make a good investment. How should our generation view the "buy a house for investment" mentality of the previous generation? Jeff Park: The rise in property prices isn't because the physical value of the houses themselves has increased, but because the dollar is constantly depreciating. If you think about it carefully, real estate is a capital expenditure that requires constant maintenance. Stones weather, and you need to pay property taxes, mortgage taxes, maintenance fees, and homeowner's insurance. Houses are essentially depreciating assets; in fact, US tax law allows real estate investors to write off depreciation within 20 to 30 years. People use homeownership as a primary form of savings because it's inextricably linked to social functions—for example, paying hefty property taxes to secure a place in a public school for your child. Currently, the real estate market faces two major problems: liquidity transformation and demographic shifts. Did you know that the average age of Americans applying for mortgages today is 59? This is clearly not first-time homebuyers, but rather people in their sixties buying their third or fourth home. This directly squeezes out young people in their mid-twenties who want to buy their first home and start a family, essentially blocking their path to family life. Furthermore, when New Yorkers move to Austin, Texas to buy homes because of high taxes, Austin locals are also angry because their home prices have been inflated by New York capital. This is a problem of capital control; young people are being completely squeezed out of the market. Host: As a rational man in my 30s, I have a job, a girlfriend, and plan to get married and have children in the future, so I need a house. But you're telling me that buying a house is even a bad investment. I only have $100,000 or $500,000 in savings, what should I do? Jeff Park: To be honest, in a core city like New York, renting is definitely a more economical choice. When you own a house, various taxes, property fees, and insurance will eat up your return on capital to less than 2% or even less than 1%. You'd be better off putting your money in a money market fund and earning a risk-free 3.5%. Buying a house is purely gambling on rising property prices. Therefore, for young people without children, renting is the most economically correct outcome. But once you have children, you need stability, you need to send your children to school, and you have to pay a high "premium" for that peace of mind. This is no longer just a simple economic calculation. This is why modern young people are reluctant to have children; once they have children, they can no longer continue renting, disrupting their entire lifecycle and creating immense pressure. In Asia, such as Japan and South Korea, another common phenomenon is that young people are anxiously waiting for the wealth of their parents to be transferred after their parents pass away. However, the lifespan of older generations is increasing, and this time lag creates significant social friction between the two generations. Host: Are we doomed to wait desperately until we're 60, hoping our parents will leave us some property? Are there any other options? Jeff Park: Yes. There is now a better way to preserve wealth than real estate. This wealth requires no service, takes up no space, requires no maintenance, and isn't taxed every time—that's Bitcoin. Bitcoin will directly alleviate pressure on the real estate market. The billionaire who went to New York to buy a $40 million penthouse to transfer $50 million can now directly buy Bitcoin. You don't need to pay huge annual maintenance fees, nor do you need to worry about the government arbitrarily confiscating your property using "expropriation rights." Once this hot money, meant for preserving value, stops flowing into real estate, the demand curve will reset, housing prices will fall, and young people will be able to afford homes. Although there will be short-term pain from falling real estate prices, it's a win-win situation for society as a whole. This is why Michael Saylor calls Bitcoin "digital real estate," just like Manhattan land 100 years ago. Capital naturally gravitates towards efficient places; if you don't give it an outlet, society will eventually collapse. Host: You mentioned "smart investors" in your article. What are "smart investors"? Why have they declined? Jeff Park: "Smart investors" refers to value investors like Buffett or Benjamin Graham, who look for stocks that are extremely cheap relative to cash flow and have low price-to-earnings ratios. But I think that era is over. Because the best-performing assets now aren't "cheap" things, but things that are "scarce" and people believe have additional value. The entire framework of the "smart investor" is built on the assumption that everything must be priced according to the "risk-free rate" (i.e., US Treasury bonds). However, due to challenges to the creditworthiness of the US government, the foundation of the risk-free rate has been shaken. This is why the traditional 60/40 (stock/bond) portfolio has failed, and why the correlation between US Treasuries and US stocks is increasing. Once you remove the risk-free rate as a valuation anchor, the market becomes a chaotic mess. Host: So what is an "ideological investor"? Jeff Park: Traditional value investors try to hedge against the influence of geopolitics, AI, and culture, seeking so-called intrinsic value. "Ideological investors," on the other hand, take a different approach. They spend a lot of time predicting the future, focusing on capital flows and paradigm shifts in liquidity. They understand that the US government is actively buying assets, so they buy what the "White House asset management company" would buy. They know how to identify asset manipulation and avoid traditional valuation traps. Host: This sounds like the job of a Wall Street Chief Investment Officer (CIO). Can you explain it in simpler terms that older women can understand? Jeff Park: Actually, older women are very good at this. They know that the truly valuable things aren't Apple stock in their securities accounts. The most valuable things might exist in the physical realm, like her unique jewelry, or the Hermès bags in her wardrobe (which have outperformed the S&P 500 for 20 years). Or some great works of art. These things, traditionally not considered financial assets, are the real tools for wealth diversification. Your financial advisor will only teach you to buy a 60/40 stock/bond portfolio, private equity, or venture capital, but it's essentially the same thing; they're all subject to the same "global arbitrage trade" and risk-free interest rates. What you need is a different pool of assets, things that macroeconomic cycles never touch—that's true decorrelated diversification. Cryptocurrencies, gold, Hermès bags, limited-edition sneakers, and Pokémon cards all fall into this category. I also believe that a major asset class of the future is "data." Young people have realized that Facebook is profiting from their data, and in the future, they will control and monetize their data themselves through decentralized technologies such as prediction markets. Wall Street advisors will never teach you how to play prediction markets, but this will be the trend because young people know that traditional financial games are manipulated, and they crave alternatives. This is why Bitcoin, DeFi, and sports betting have emerged.

Host: Macroeconomist Raoul Pal said, "Diversification is dead," and that the performance of all assets is only related to money printing and fiat currency devaluation, so he's all in cryptocurrencies. What do you think?

Jeff Park: I both agree and disagree. If you're only focusing on traditional assets manipulated by the same set of global liquidity, then diversification is indeed meaningless. But if you broaden your perspective and focus on investment categories that are not manipulated by this flow of funds, diversification still has value.

Last year, I proposed the "Aggressive Portfolio Theory," listing 25 different uncorrelated assets. For example, gold, in Asian culture, remains the most primitive and irreplaceable store of value. Great works of art; during the 2008 financial crisis, the best trades were actually in the art asset class. There are also those trading scarce, high-end wines. I'm very optimistic about "tokenization" in the cryptocurrency space, but my initial intention wasn't to tokenize BlackRock's funds, but rather to tokenize high-barrier-to-entry long-tail alternative assets like wine and superyachts. This way, even without millions, ordinary people can buy a small portion of this yacht for $100, achieving a risk-averse portfolio like that of billionaires. Host: But this is still too complicated for ordinary people. For example, my 35-year-old sister is just an ordinary office worker; how can she accumulate wealth? Jeff Park: Over the past 20 years, young people have become more financially savvy. Don't laugh when you see young people trading limited-edition sneakers and Pokémon trading cards. This is precisely the diversified wealth thinking that young people need, instead of blindly chasing Nvidia stock. Young people are playing their own game, and if they succeed in it, that's incredibly powerful. Host: Now there's something else that's causing people to lose their jobs: AI. You wrote an article called "Occupy AI." Could you explain what "Occupy Wall Street" is before talking about "Occupy AI"? Jeff Park: There was the "Occupy Wall Street" movement in 2008, where angry people camped out in downtown New York City demanding justice. During the subprime mortgage crisis, banks were morally and legally at fault, privatizing profits while socializing losses (getting taxpayers to bail them out) without facing consequences. I believe AI will trigger a potentially even more extreme class struggle. We've never encountered a technology as disruptive as AI, capable of completely replacing labor while generating record profits for corporate executives. We will see a more extreme K-shaped economy, where corporate profits rise not because of increased revenue, but because of cost-cutting through layoffs. As I wrote in my article, "Amazon laid off 30,000 people while hitting record highs in the stock market, which illustrates the collapse of the price of free will and the soaring value of self-determination." Host: Can you explain what this means? Jeff Park: People work not just to earn money, but also to be productive, contribute to society, and set an example for their children. If people don't create value, they will have psychological problems. Past technological advancements (such as electricity, cars, trains, and email) have amplified human capabilities, allowing you to work faster and better, but the key is that you are still working. But some aspects of AI completely eliminate work. Even more disturbing is that, to support the construction of AI data centers, some are calling for government backing, packaging it as a survival crisis of "if we don't do it, other countries will." So governments use funds to invest in technologies that will precisely replace the jobs of their taxpayers. Do you think the public will support this self-funded, self-destructive plan? That's why an "Occupy AI" is inevitable. Unlike the explicit enemies on Wall Street dressed in suits and Hermès ties, AI is invisible. Tech giants will only say, "We're just a platform." Today's young people graduate with huge student loans, can't find jobs, can't afford houses, and may never have jobs again. Meanwhile, tech giants are each other's clients, funds are flowing between Microsoft, OpenAI, Anthropic, and Nvidia, creating new highs in the US stock market—this is absolutely abnormal. Host: You wrote at the end of your article, "Occupy Wall Street turned millennials into die-hard Bitcoin supporters, while Occupy AI will turn Generation Z and Alpha into Bitcoin supporters." Can you explain this in simple terms? Jeff Park: Everyone needs a moment of "awakening" or "enlightenment" to discover Bitcoin. For many millennials, this moment of epiphany came during the 2008 financial crisis and the COVID-19 pandemic, when we realized the existing monetary system was a scam. But for Generation Z and Alpha, currency devaluation no longer holds much appeal. They're already disillusioned and know the system is beyond saving. And with institutions like BlackRock buying up Bitcoin in large quantities, they even feel it's become "old people's game." But I believe AI will be the catalyst for their awakening. This generation will be competing with AI for jobs right out of school, which touches on their deepest personal interests. They'll find Bitcoin to be the best safe-haven asset. And if they feel AI is having a significant negative social impact, they'll vote with their feet. Both AI and Bitcoin are extremely energy-intensive, so they might choose to support Bitcoin. More importantly, the core of AI is centralization; it collects your data and uses it to replace you. If your data is used to train smarter models, you must be compensated. The only theoretical way to achieve this ownership traceability and value distribution is through decentralized encryption. This might inspire the younger generation to rediscover the "decentralized" nature of cryptocurrencies. I remain optimistic about AI benefiting society, but only if the issue of "data contribution compensation" is resolved. Host: Many people might feel that Bitcoin is currently fluctuating at $60,000, $70,000, or even hundreds of thousands of dollars, which is too expensive, and they've missed their last buying opportunity. What are your thoughts on this? Jeff Park: I think people need to consider: what downside risks do you face if you don't hold Bitcoin? If you don't hold Bitcoin, you're essentially shorting it. Fiat currencies are depreciating at an unprecedented rate. Looking back, when the dollar's fiscal deficit reached an uncontrollable escape velocity, you had to include the "fastest horse" in your portfolio—that is, Bitcoin—or assets like Hermès or Rolex that resist global arbitrage cycles. Host: As a CIO who emphasizes diversification, do you advise those who hold large amounts of Bitcoin in their portfolios as savings to adopt a defensive or offensive approach? Jeff Park: Many people in the industry adopt a "barbell strategy," holding half Bitcoin and half money market funds (cash), and nothing else. Personally, I prefer a more diversified asset allocation. But if I were forced to keep only two assets in my portfolio, my choices would be: first, Bitcoin, because it's the least correlated asset with global capital markets; second, you still need interest-bearing assets in the dollar system. For example, I believe we will eventually return to a zero-interest-rate environment (maintaining the global leverage game through interest rate cuts), so buying 30-year US Treasury bonds now is a good investment opportunity because bond prices rise when interest rates fall. This is essentially betting that America's innovative capabilities will eventually solve the problem. Host: How do you use the Bitcoin mindset to educate your two children to face the AI-dominated world of the future? Jeff Park: Bitcoin taught me one thing: always stay open and humble, because the world is bigger than any individual or model. It's a living experiment. A phrase I often tell my children is that it's not practice that creates perfection, but practice that creates progress. Bitcoin will never be absolutely perfect, nothing will ever be perfect, but we can continuously improve in the pursuit of our ideals.

data.title: Is the Fed Chair-elect really pro-crypto?
data.description:
Author: Thejaswini M A; Translator: BitpushNews

Before Kevin Warsh could head the Federal Reserve, he had to sell his holdings in Solana, dYdX, Optimism, Polymarket, Dapper Labs, and about twenty other companies. This is an odd prerequisite for a job, but it's a legal requirement and applies equally to anyone hoping to regulate the U.S. financial system, regardless of their understanding of it.

The disclosure that triggered all of this arrived on April 14. The 69-page document, submitted to the Office of Government Ethics, cleared the final administrative hurdle before his confirmation hearings.

The document is a standard compliance form. At the same time, if you know where to look, it's one of the most influential document records ever seen by Senate committees.

Walsh and his wife, Jane Lauder (whose family interests include Estée Lauder cosmetics, with a net worth estimated at around $1.9 billion by Forbes), hold combined assets worth at least $192 million. The majority of this is in positions held by two Juggernaut Fund LPs, each worth over $50 million, related to his advisory work for Stanley Druckenmiller's Duquesne Family Office. The underlying assets are protected by confidentiality agreements. Certification officials from the Government Ethics Office specifically flagged these assets and confirmed that Walsh will comply with federal ethics laws once he divests the assets he intends to hold. The portfolio encompasses decentralized finance (DeFi) protocols, Ethereum scaling networks, Bitcoin payment infrastructure, and prediction markets. It reads like he's scouted the entire crypto industry and deliberately bought a bit of everything in each category. Through AVGF I, Walsh indirectly holds stakes in Solana, Optimism, and the Lightning Network. Through DCM Investments 10 LLC, disclosures list dYdX, Polychain Capital, Compound, Blast, Lighter, and Lemon Cash. Another AVF fund series includes Dapper Labs, DeSo, Friends With Benefits, and Zero Gravity. He also directly holds shares in Web3 gaming company Metatheory, valued between $1,000 and $15,000. Flashnet, a Bitcoin merchant payments startup, is also included. Polymarket is also in the portfolio. These cryptocurrency positions are mostly within fund instruments, where individual projects are not reported in US dollars. According to the Government Office of Ethics, this means each item is valued at less than $1,000, representing small-scale venture capital investments rather than concentrated holdings. Size isn't the key factor; breadth is. This portfolio covers L1 blockchains, L2 scaling solutions, DeFi lending, decentralized derivatives, NFT infrastructure, Bitcoin payments, and prediction markets. The only missing categories are memes, game tokens, mining companies, and directly held Bitcoin. Everything he owns falls into the categories of infrastructure, financial pipelines, or developer tools. What does the money say? What Warsh's money represents is that cryptocurrency is the next layer of financial infrastructure, built on top of protocols that process real transactions, and what the people building these protocols are doing, in a way, very similar to fintech. The closest thing to a speculative bet in the entire disclosure is Friends With Benefits, a social token community. But considering all the other projects in the portfolio, it's more like someone invited him to dinner, and he politely wrote a small check. His past public statements align with this. In 2011, Warsh saw the Bitcoin white paper at a dinner hosted by Marc Andreessen. In 2018, he wrote in the Wall Street Journal that Bitcoin could become a sustainable store of value, similar to gold. In 2021, he stated on CNBC that Bitcoin is the "new gold" for people under 40. In 2025, at the Hoover Institution, he published his most comprehensive argument to date: Bitcoin is not a substitute for the dollar, but it can serve as an excellent "watchdog" of monetary policy. I wouldn't call Walsh a Bitcoin maximalist. A monetary policy professional who sees Bitcoin as a legitimate signal of the dollar's health might, in some ways, be more useful to the crypto industry.

Wash earned $10.2 million in consulting fees from the Duquesne Family Office, the investment arm of Stanley Druckenmiller, one of the world's most reputable macro investors, and one of the few who have seriously discussed the crypto space at this level.

It was Druckenmiller who told Morgan Stanley last month that stablecoins would become the payment system across the United States within 10 to 15 years. Then, in the same interview, he described the rest of the crypto space as "finding solutions to problems."

It was Druckenmiller who told Morgan Stanley last month that stablecoins would become the payment system across the United States within 10 to 15 years. Then, in the same interview, he said that the rest of the crypto space was about "finding solutions to problems."

... Warsh's crypto exposure flows through the same network as arguably the world's most reputable macro investor. Druckenmiller says stablecoins are the future and everything else is noise, and Warsh's portfolio is filled with the infrastructure that makes stablecoins work—this is starting to seem like a common argument. Warsh has pledged to divest all assets. Selling liquidity token positions is fairly straightforward. But unwinding LP stakes in Polychain Capital or venture capital fund positions in illiquid early-stage companies is much more difficult. Some funds with multiple LPs are not typically required to divest under Government Ethics Office (GEO) rules. But GEO certification officials specifically flagged Juggernaut Fund's positions and made full divestment a condition of compliance. The underlying assets in these positions remain undisclosed due to existing confidentiality agreements. Federal ethics rules typically require a one-year "cooling-off period" for matters that directly impact near-term financial interests. This could become crucial as the Federal Reserve weighs its options on stablecoin legislation, tokenized deposits and securities, and research into central bank digital currencies (CBDCs). Consider what this means in practice. Congress is currently debating stablecoin frameworks. The CLARITY Act is still progressing in the Senate. Banks are running pilot programs for tokenized deposits. The Federal Reserve plays a continuous role in all of this. And the person running the Fed—the one who has actually invested in DeFi protocols, Bitcoin payment infrastructure, and prediction markets—may sit on the sidelines for the first year, while his colleagues who have never used a crypto wallet are involved in decision-making. It would be incomplete to write about Warsh's cryptocurrency holdings without mentioning the context of his nomination. The Trump family's crypto businesses had already generated substantial returns before any crypto legislation was signed. According to the Wall Street Journal, World Liberty Financial—a DeFi project that lists Barron Trump as a "DeFi visionary" on its website—has already generated at least $1.2 billion in realized returns for the Trump family by early 2026. The executive order allowing 401(k) accounts to invest in cryptocurrencies was signed by a president whose family is simultaneously cashing out from crypto businesses. Warsh's holdings are small-scale venture investments through a fund structure. The Trump family's holdings are concentrated, large positions that directly benefit from federal policy. The two are different in size and structure. Their connection lies in this: a president who financially benefits from a cryptocurrency-friendly Federal Reserve has chosen a nominee for Fed chair who has a financial interest in the crypto industry he will be regulating. Whether this represents blatant corruption, strategic alignment, or simply a natural evolution of American institutions depends on perspective. And that perspective often depends on whether you happen to own the relevant assets yourself. Senator Thom Tillis has stated he will oppose any Fed nominee until the Justice Department's investigation into Powell reaches a fully transparent conclusion. The committee is partisan, meaning that confirmation could be delayed if even one Republican defectes. Jerome Powell's term as Federal Reserve Chairman will end on May 15. If Walsh is not confirmed by then, Powell will remain in a caretaker role, while oil prices are above $100, the situation in Iran remains unresolved, and the market is trying to determine whether the Fed will have a leader. For the crypto community, Walsh's asset disclosure is a classic "good news, bad news" joke. What's the good news? We finally have a Fed Chairman who can distinguish between a "liquidity pool" and a "swimming pool." What's the bad news? Because he knows "how sausages are made" (deep inside knowledge), federal ethics laws have effectively cornered him in a digital corner, leaving him to "hang out" for a full year—watching his colleagues, who "don't own a single coin," try to regulate a technology they probably think is a sequel to *The Matrix*. These two things can be true simultaneously. He understands DeFi from an internal perspective, and also believes that the Fed's primary responsibility is monetary stability, while asset price inflation is a symptom of policy failure. These views are destined to make future FOMC meetings very interesting. Warsh's disclosure document is less a signal of a systemic shift and more a quiet acknowledgment of path dependence. Our past choices and technological evolution inevitably constrain the future. For decades, the Fed has been operating on a traditional track. And as the monetary architecture shifts from paper-based to protocol-based, this "path" naturally widens. This is a slow and steady process: integrating new competencies into the existing system. It signifies an awareness—to govern the future of the dollar, one must first understand the language that is writing that future.

data.title: Liang Wenfeng's First-Ever Funding Round – DeepSeek V4 Completely Breaks Free from Nvidia
data.description:
This morning, the AI ​​community was in an uproar! DeepSeek, which has never sought funding, has spoken out for the first time: seeking a $10 billion valuation and raising $300 million. With V4 on the horizon, boasting trillions of parameters and backed by Huawei's Ascend, even Huang (Jensen Huang, CEO of Huawei) is worried! This morning, the AI ​​community was shocked by this news. The Information reported that DeepSeek is seeking its first external funding round! Breaking its "never seeking funding" rule, DeepSeek is now seeking at least $300 million at a valuation of at least $10 billion. In the extremely expensive race to develop large AI models, DeepSeek needs to replenish its financial arsenal. DeepSeek, once a staunch advocate of not seeking funding, has repeatedly rejected investment offers from top domestic venture capital firms and tech giants after its R1 round caused a stir in Silicon Valley and Wall Street. This latest fundraising move by DeepSeek signifies a major shift in its management philosophy, as evidenced by Liang Wenfeng's (Huanfang Quant) decision. Previously, as a technological idealist, he had always hoped to maintain DeepSeek's independence, free from commercial pressure. The last time a new generation model was released was in 2025 when DeepSeek R1 became a sensation. Now, DeepSeek has kept the entire industry waiting for a year and a half. If this funding round is successful, DeepSeek can acquire more computing power and offer higher salaries to prevent the loss of top researchers. However, due to DeepSeek's status as a "Chinese startup," some American venture capitalists are more cautious. The release of DeepSeek V4 has been repeatedly delayed, and the competitive landscape of the AI ​​industry has changed dramatically. Currently, the world's leading models are rapidly iterating, and tech giants from China and the US are leveraging their deep pockets to continuously gain a leading position. Perhaps this pressure has finally prompted DeepSeek to change its funding strategy.

V4, a tough battle with trillions of parameters

According to The Information, V4 was originally scheduled to debut in February of this year, but has been delayed multiple times. Reuters' latest timeframe in early April is "within the next few weeks."

V4, a tough battle with trillions of parameters

Based on currently available information, V4's scale and ambition far surpass its predecessor. The number of parameters has jumped to the trillion level. V4 adopts the MoE architecture, with a total of approximately one trillion parameters, but each token only activates about 37 billion parameters, keeping inference costs on par with V3. This design philosophy continues DeepSeek's consistent efficiency-first approach. The context window of the leaked image from the source network has been expanded to 1 million tokens. V4 introduces a conditional memory architecture called Engram, achieving constant-time retrieval of extremely long contexts. According to internal testing, the information recall rate reached 97% with a token length of 1 million, far exceeding V3's performance with 128K contexts. Native multimodal. According to the FT, V4 will be DeepSeek's first native multimodal model, supporting text, image, and video generation. Previously, DeepSeek's models were primarily text-based, while other flagship models had already embraced multimodal capabilities. Significantly enhanced code generation capabilities. From its inception, V4 has focused on code generation as a core objective. Internal benchmarks show SWE-bench scores exceeding 80% and HumanEval scores reaching 90%. It's claimed that V4 can handle complex bug fixes at the entire codebase level, and its performance in long-context code inference will surpass the Claude and GPT series. Furthermore, there are reports that V4 is expected to be released in two versions: a full version with over a trillion parameters, optimized for advanced inference and complex code tasks, targeting Huawei's Ascend chip; and a lightweight version with approximately 200 billion parameters, targeting general dialogue and API services, capable of running on other domestically produced chips. Regarding open source, V4 is planned to be released under the Apache 2.0 license, continuing DeepSeek's consistent open-source strategy. When will it be available? Multiple sources point to the end of April. On March 9th, a version called "V4 Lite" briefly appeared on the DeepSeek platform before being removed. In early April, developers discovered a test version of V4 Lite on API nodes, showing a 30% improvement in inference speed and a jump in information recall rate from 45% to 94% for 128K context. DeepSeek recently posted job openings for server maintenance engineers and delivery managers in Ulanqab, Inner Mongolia. This marks the company's first public recruitment of on-site personnel related to computing infrastructure. All indications suggest that V4 is no longer in the lab but is undergoing final preparations for large-scale deployment. The thing Huang (DeepSeek's founder) least wanted to see is happening. The core reason for V4's delay has little to do with the model itself. The real challenge lies in a major migration of underlying hardware. Previously, all of DeepSeek's models were trained on NVIDIA chips. But V4 has taken a different path. According to Reuters, V4 will run on Huawei's latest Ascend chip. DeepSeek engineers have spent considerable time resolving V4's compatibility issues with Huawei chips, rewriting core code, and migrating from NVIDIA's CUDA ecosystem to Huawei's CANN architecture. This is both a technical decision and a strategic signal. DeepSeek deliberately did not provide NVIDIA and AMD with V4 for optimization and adaptation in advance, instead exclusively granting early access to domestic chip manufacturers. If V4 achieves competitive performance on Huawei chips, it will be the world's first cutting-edge AI model that doesn't rely on Nvidia. Huang himself is not calm about this. In a recent interview, he bluntly stated that DeepSeek's new model based on the Huawei platform "will be a bad outcome for the United States." The implication is that once AI models are optimized to run best on Chinese hardware, the moat of American chips will no longer be solid. $300 million, breaking away from the Nvidia ecosystem? Even DeepSeek, known for its "high-performance with low budgets," cannot rely solely on one company for continuous funding. According to Stanford University's 2026 AI Index report, as of March this year, the performance gap between top American models and the strongest Chinese competitors was only 2.7 percentage points. The smaller the gap, the higher the marginal cost of each step of catching up. Just this month, OpenAI completed a $40 billion funding round at a $300 billion valuation; compared to that, $300 million is not an astronomical figure. The real gamble for V4 lies in proving that cutting-edge AI can operate completely independently of the Nvidia ecosystem. $300 million is staked on this.

data.title: What industries and startups did Claude Design's release actually destroy?
data.description:
ALAN WALKER · 4.17.2026 · PALO ALTO

Silicon Valley OG Alan Walker, who has worked for three acquired companies, invested in numerous projects, and witnessed countless failures.

Today is Friday, April 17, 2026. Alan is sitting in Zombie Café, finally finishing his third latte. He did one thing—he read Anthropic Labs' press release about Claude Design twice, repeatedly rewound the demo video, mentally reviewed the competing products on the market, and then typed out this article.

This is not a review, it's an obituary.

PRODUCT

Claude Design is a new product launched by Anthropic Labs today, running on the visual model of Claude Opus 4.7.

In Silicon Valley terms—you describe what you want in the chat box, and Claude creates it for you: design drafts, interactive prototypes, pitch decks, landing pages, and even cutting-edge interactive prototypes with shaders, 3D, voice, and video. The whole package. Your brand system is automatically read from the codebase and Figma files; modifications are made via chat, inline comments, or adjustments generated on-the-spot by Claude; once finished, it can be directly exported as PPTX, PDF, or HTML, or packaged and handed over to Claude Code for implementation. Canva is an official partner; Datadog and Brilliant are launch partners. Pro, Max, Team, and Enterprise subscriptions include it directly. What's New About It? For the past three years, the entire "AI design" field has essentially been doing the same thing: wrapping an LLM layer in a Figma plugin layer or a standalone app. Gamma is an LLM package for slides. Galileo is an LLM package for UI mockups. v0 is an LLM package for React. They are all wrappers. Claude Design is the first—and likely the last—product to put the entire workflow (system onboarding → exploration → refinement → collaboration → export → project delivery) end-to-end into the native LLM runtime. This isn't "adding AI," it's using AI as the foundation. It sounds like a slogan, but the commercial difference is orders of magnitude. 01 Design System Automatically Generated from Codebase The original press release stated: During onboarding, Claude builds a design system for your team by reading your codebase and design files. This strikes at the heart of design system tools: where does the truth come from? For the past decade, the industry has accepted the assumption that a design system is an independently maintained asset, requiring synchronization, documentation, and governance. This assumption is wrong. The design system should inherently reside within the code. Components, tokens, and runtime are all within the code. The Figma library is merely a projection, and the DS SaaS is simply copy management. Claude directly reads the codebase, extracting consistency. The disappearance of copies → the disappearance of conflicts → the governance layer becomes meaningless. The budget that enterprises previously spent on token alignment will be directly transferred to Anthropic's subscription revenue.

02 A prompt generates the first version of the design

The original statement in the press release:
Describe what you need and Claude builds a first version.

This doesn't change efficiency, but the cost function:

In the past: time was expensive, directions were limited
Now: time is almost free, directions are unlimited

The bottleneck in design exploration is not refinement, but insufficient samples.
AI gives you 10+ directions at once, and designers go from "drawing" to "judging".

All products that use "AI-generated UI" as their core selling point have had their essential value swallowed up.

03 ​​Static mockups become interactive prototypes, no PR needed

The original statement in the press release:
Turn static mockups into interactive prototypes without code review.

This is a process-level break.

The reason for the existence of prototyping tools in the past: to connect design and engineering.

Now: Prototypes are directly running code.

The boundary between prototype and product disappears. The intermediate layer (prototyping tools) loses its basis for existence. 04 Deck, Export, Canva Press release quote: From outline to complete deck in minutes. Deck is essentially structured content generation, a task LLM excels at. All AI deck products exist for one reason: Claude hadn't done it yet. Now Claude has. 05 Marketing Materials and Landing Page Press release quote: Create landing pages and campaign visuals. The core KPI for marketing is: how many variations can be tested each week. Claude compresses the entire process to near real-time. The key is not "being able to generate pages," but "generating them using your own design system." Brand consistency changes from manual proofreading to automatic forced alignment.

06 Frontier Design: 3D, Voice, Video

Press release quote:
Anyone can build code-powered prototypes.

“Anyone” is key.

What used to require multi-role teams can now be done by a single PM in half an hour.

The technology stack is no longer a barrier.

07 Handoff Bundle → Claude Code

Press release quote:
Package everything into a handoff bundle.

The translation layer from design to code disappears.

Design and code become two views of the same object, not two systems.

There is no handoff, only render.

08 Web Capture

Press release quote:
Grab real elements from your website.

The gap between prototypes and real products is essentially a “fake UI.”

Claude directly captures real UI elements. Prototype = Real Product. Conclusion: Claude Design is not a tool. Anthropic integrates the entire creative workflow (exploration, collaboration, delivery) into its runtime. Figma can survive because it is a social network and identity system. But all "middleware tools around Figma" must re-prove their existence. Conclusion for investors: When giants have done all the create + iterate + export + handoff, entrepreneurs are left with only two paths: 1) What giants can't do: compliance, highly sensitive industries (healthcare, finance, government) 2) What giants don't do: long tail, localization, small markets. Middleware is eliminated. This is the norm after platform-level release. Last year it was writing; the year before it was customer service; this year it's design. Who's next? It will continue. Every technological revolution is the same: It's not the early adopters who make money; it's the ones who understand it first.

data.title: Golden Web3.0 Daily Report | Ethereum Foundation Core Member Announces Departure
data.description:
DeFi data

1. Total market value of DeFi tokens: US$55.926 billion

DeFi Total Market Cap Data Source: coingecko

2. Trading volume of decentralized exchanges in the past 24 hours: $52.73

Trading volume of decentralized exchanges in the past 24 hours. Data source: coingecko

3. Assets locked in DeFi: $982.4 billion Top 10 DeFi Projects by Locked Assets and Total Value Locked (Data Source: defillama)

Top 10 DeFi Projects by Locked Assets and Total Value Locked (Data Source: defillama)

NFT Data

1. Total Market Value of NFTs: $8.123 Billion

alt="vbH8xdA1Z39LNYjrkHYgkacfxF0b3aGxuCi9qJHz.png">

NFT Total Market Capitalization, Top Ten Projects by Market Capitalization Data Source: Coinmarketcap

2.24-hour NFT transaction volume: $2.246 billionUSD

    AI Hot Topics

    1. Xiaomi miclaw Passes First Batch of Trusted AI Mobile Assistant (Claw) Evaluation by the China Academy of Information and Communications Technology

    Xiaomi miclaw recently officially passed the mobile assistant (Claw) evaluation by the China Academy of Information and Communications Technology, becoming the first mobile intelligent agent in China to pass this review. This evaluation was based on the "General Framework for Benchmark Testing of Intelligent Assistants," and rigorously evaluated from three dimensions: basic capabilities, edge applications, and comprehensive capabilities. According to reports, Xiaomi miclaw relies on Xiaomi MiMo's self-developed large model and possesses four major capabilities: full ecosystem underlying support, deep memory, cross-domain interconnection, and continuous self-evolution. It can connect mobile phones, PCs, cockpits, and AIoT devices to autonomously complete complex commands.

    2. Anthropic CEO to Meet White House Chief of Staff on Friday On April 17, the CEO of Anthropic will meet with the White House Chief of Staff at the White House. 3. OpenAI Employment Research Report: AI May Increase Jobs Rather Than Cause Massive Unemployment On April 17, an OpenAI study of over 900 occupations showed that the shockwaves caused by AI in the labor market may not be as tragic as many expect. The report points out that although jobs such as data entry, bookkeeping, and customer service face extremely high risks of automation (accounting for approximately 18% of total employment), workers in these fields are already using AI to handle about three times the workload of other occupations, and their unemployment rates are rising at a lower rate than those of lower-risk occupations. This counterintuitive phenomenon stems from "consumption elasticity": when AI makes a task (such as coding) cheaper and faster, the total market demand for that service often grows exponentially, offsetting the downsizing pressure from increased efficiency. The report categorizes professions into four groups: excluding the high-risk group mentioned above, 46% of professions (such as teachers and domestic workers) are minimally affected; 24% of roles may shrink in size but still require human leadership; and 12% of professions (such as software development) will see job expansion due to the widespread adoption of AI. Currently, practitioners in high-risk professions utilize less than a quarter of the theoretical capabilities of AI.

    4. ChatGPT's global weekly active users approach 1 billion, with female users exceeding males by over 50%

    On April 17th, OpenAI released the latest data showing a historic shift in the structure of its ChatGPT user base.

    When ChatGPT was launched in 2022, male users accounted for approximately 80% and female users for approximately 20%; currently, female users have surpassed male users, accounting for over 50%. According to a blog post cited by IT Home, based on ChatGPT's current weekly active users, this means that nearly 500 million women worldwide are regularly using the tool, and the total number of ChatGPT users is about to reach 1 billion. 5. Cyberspace Administration of China: Strengthening Legislation in Artificial Intelligence, Digital Economy, Blockchain, and Other Fields On April 17, the Cyberspace Administration of China published an article entitled "Building a Rule-of-Law Cyberspace that Benefits the People," which pointed out the need to continuously improve laws and regulations that promote and protect the development of informatization, especially strengthening legislation in areas such as artificial intelligence, digital economy, network infrastructure, satellite internet, and blockchain, striving to ensure that wherever informatization development goes, cyber legislation covers, using the rule of law to safeguard technological innovation and self-reliance in the cyberspace field.

    DeFi Hot Topics

    1. Osmosis's proposal to integrate with Cosmos Hub fails to pass vote; it will continue to operate independently

    Osmosis announced that its governance proposal to integrate Osmosis into Cosmos Hub failed to pass the vote by a narrow margin. The team stated that although this result is not the best solution desired by the Cosmos ecosystem, they still thank the community, validators, and all members who participated in the discussion. Osmosis emphasized that its network will continue to operate as an independent and profitable chain. In the coming weeks, the team will move forward with the next phase of Osmosis's planning, prioritizing user asset security and service continuity. Further arrangements for the protocol and team development will be announced later.

    2. Ethereum Foundation-funded project Ketman identifies 100 North Korean IT personnel. On April 17th, the Ethereum Foundation announced that its funded Ketman project identified 100 North Korean IT personnel lurking within Web3 organizations within six months and issued warnings to approximately 53 projects, indicating they might have employed such individuals. This project focuses on the issue of "fake developers" in the crypto industry and is part of the ETHRangers public security funding program. Ketman has also developed an open-source detection tool for identifying suspicious GitHub activity and is collaborating with the Security Alliance to develop an industry identification framework for identifying North Korean IT personnel. 3. DoubleZero launches Edge platform, providing low-latency on-chain data services. The DoubleZero Foundation announced that its Edge platform has officially entered the public testing phase. Edge is a permissionless platform running on DoubleZero's DePIN infrastructure, designed to provide ultra-low latency, real-time data delivery services for on-chain markets. DoubleZero is committed to building a decentralized global fiber optic network to optimize low-latency distributed systems for blockchains. DoubleZero co-founder Andrew McConnell stated, "Traditional finance gains a competitive advantage through decades of building speed and deterministic infrastructure, while on-chain markets previously lacked a similar foundation. Edge aims to provide on-chain traders with the same level of deterministic infrastructure, enabling tighter spreads and better execution." Currently, Edge has approximately 350 data providers, and major Solana infrastructure providers such as Jito, Temporal, Staking Facilities, and Triton have also joined.

    4. Decibel, incubated by Aptos Labs, launches gold (XAU) perpetual contract

    On April 17th, Decibel, a perpetual contract exchange incubated by Aptos Labs, launched its first non-crypto asset perpetual contract: XAU

    5. Tempo launches privacy solution Zones, supporting permissioned parallel blockchain operation

    On April 17th, Tempo, a Layer 1 blockchain jointly developed by Stripe and Paradigm, launched a privacy feature called Zones. This is a solution for running a permissioned parallel blockchain, designed for enterprise-level use cases such as payroll, fund management, and payment settlement, where higher confidentiality is required.

    ... It is understood that Zones, as a parallel blockchain connected to the Tempo mainnet, provide a private execution environment. Internal transactions remain private, but assets and funds are fully interconnected with Tempo L1, other Zones, deposit channels, and liquidity pools. Each Zone is managed by a trusted entity such as a financial institution or infrastructure provider. Operators can view all activities within their environment and implement access controls, but do not control the underlying assets. Disclaimer: Jinse Finance, as a blockchain information platform, publishes articles for informational purposes only and does not constitute actual investment advice. Please establish correct investment concepts and be sure to enhance your risk awareness.

data.title: When active market makers begin to take initiative
data.description:
Author: Gu Yu, ChainCatcher

While mainstream cryptocurrencies stabilized and rebounded, the altcoin market suddenly changed dramatically, with RAVE, ORDI, Binance Life, and other tokens experiencing significant surges. RAVE, in particular, saw a peak increase of 86 times.

It's worth noting that this wasn't just a few tokens rising, but a collective surge driven by hundreds of tokens. This is extremely rare in the bear market of the past year.

According to RootData, as of 12:00 PM on April 17th, among tokens with a circulating market capitalization exceeding $10 million, over 45 tokens saw gains exceeding 50% in the past seven days, and over 170 tokens saw gains exceeding 20%, representing 19.2% of all tokens with a circulating market capitalization exceeding $10 million.

If we extend the timeframe to one month, at least three projects achieved a maximum increase of 10 times: RAVE, ARIA, and Binance Life. Further analysis of these high-growth tokens reveals the following characteristics: First, they are primarily tokens issued within the last year. For example, RAVE, Binance Life, and GENIUS were all issued since October of last year. Statistics show that over two-thirds of these tokens were issued within the last year. Due to the high concentration of holdings and the fact that VC token releases have not yet begun, project teams and market makers can easily buy back tokens at low prices, creating favorable conditions for manipulating price charts and institutional investors. Second, the focus is on AI and meme-related tokens, such as ORDI, SIREN, and KSYAI. These tokens also largely lack VC investment and holdings, have stronger community consensus, and are all based on recent popular narratives, making them more likely to drive investor sentiment. Third, most of these high-growth tokens are listed on the Binance Futures market, and without any positive news, they often use sudden surges (over 50%) as a starting point, indicating significant manipulation in their price increases. In the second half of last year, Binance Alpha also saw a sustained surge in some tokens, with many newly listed Alpha tokens experiencing gains of several times their initial value. During this process, the crucial role of "active market makers" was gradually recognized by the market. In fact, Binance Alpha was a structured game orchestrated by market makers. Alpha provides the initial accumulation of tokens and initial liquidity, Perp amplifies liquidity and volatility, while OI and funding rates become key tools for market manipulators. As more and more tokens with high gains plummeted by over 90%, causing heavy losses for investors, Binance announced in late March that it would crack down on market maker irregularities, requiring token projects to disclose the identity, legal entity, and contract terms of market makers to Binance, prohibiting profit-sharing agreements and guaranteed return arrangements. However, in retrospect, this crackdown has actually made market makers more "proactive." Taking advantage of the easing of tensions in Iran and the recovery of mainstream cryptocurrencies, proactive market makers have begun actively pumping up prices after long-term accumulation, creating one speculative coin after another. The disastrous consequences created by market makers have prompted many KOLs to warn investors about market risks. “Previously, altcoin booms were driven by a collective surge of retail investor funds and widespread participation, fueled by genuine market enthusiasm. Now, the so-called 'altcoin rally' is a performance by a few coins under heavy manipulation by large investors. These large investors control liquidity, create information asymmetry, and pump and dump prices entirely at their whim. The two are fundamentally different, and blindly following the trend will only make you a victim of these large investors,” crypto KOL Shen Xiaowang posted on X. Meanwhile, a recent report by Novora founder Connor King, titled “2026 Crypto Industry Investor Relations and Token Transparency Status Report,” shows that only 1% of projects disclosed specific market makers and terms, including token lending, option structures, and performance incentives. This means that the market-making status of most projects remains opaque, with a severe lack of transparency. This frenzy has now lasted for nearly a week, with most of the high-flying tokens still hovering at high prices. Only a few tokens, such as ARIA, have seen significant price drops—ARIA's price has fallen from a high of $1.02 to $0.1. Regardless of the hidden risks, exchanges should welcome this phenomenon. According to crypto KOL Ru7, from a rational business perspective, exchanges should welcome these "monster coins," as they contribute significant transaction fees and generate buzz. The essence of these "monster coins" is to artificially create volatility and attention. "Exchanges can't completely ban 'monster' coins. Their main revenue comes from transaction fees. Without hot topics, mainstream coins fluctuate daily, with volatility not exceeding 5%. Without active market makers (MMs) creating new targets, trading frequency will definitely be even lower. Without new targets, without sentiment, without a story, the market will quickly enter a cycle of 'low volatility → low trading → low revenue,'" Ru7 said. Currently, most altcoins are still at historical lows. A 20% increase is insignificant compared to a drop of over 90%. For example, although ARB has recently risen by over 50%, it still needs to rise another 20 times to surpass its previous all-time high. Whether this is a "pseudo-altcoin season" or the early stage of a long altcoin cycle still depends on the mainstream coin market, the macroeconomic environment, and the actions of key players. Bitmine Chairman Thomas "Tom" Lee stated, "Bitmine has maintained an accelerated pace of ETH purchases every week for the past four weeks because our fundamental assessment is that ETH is in the final stages of a 'mini cryptocurrency winter.' Last week, we acquired 71,524 ETH, the highest purchase rate since the week of December 22, 2025." Crypto analyst Amr Taha pointed out that multiple indicators collectively reflect a shift in Bitcoin holdings from weaker to stronger players, showing a steady accumulation rather than aggressive selling. CoinGlass liquidity graphs show a significant concentration of visible liquidity in the $86,000 to $90,000 range. Market sentiment has turned bullish, with traders setting a Bitcoin price target of $88,000. If past altcoin surges were "capital-driven rallies," the current ones are closer to "structure-driven rallies." With a combination of high control, low liquidity, and high leverage, both price increases and decreases are amplified—and by the same logic, risks are amplified simultaneously. When the vast majority of tokens are still mired in historical declines, localized surges resemble selective liquidity releases rather than a full-blown correction. In such a market, chasing price increases is often easier than predicting trends, but the cost is also higher.

data.title: Who is creating this localized frenzy for altcoins?
data.description:
Author: Nancy

The continuous rise over the past few days has suddenly enlivened the long-dormant altcoin market. Some tokens have seen consecutive surges, even experiencing violent price increases of several times or even dozens of times in a short period, seemingly signaling the restart of the altcoin season.

However, this is not a signal of a full-scale recovery in altcoins, but rather a localized frenzy dominated by a few strong tokens.

The era of a broad-based rally is over; strong tokens take over the altcoin market.

The altcoin market has not witnessed the broad-based rally that the market had long anticipated.

According to data from CoinGecko tracking the top 1000 tokens by market capitalization, the top 30 performing tokens over the past 7 days saw an average increase of 153.5%, significantly outperforming mainstream assets such as Bitcoin, Ethereum, and Solana. These gains were primarily concentrated in memes, inscriptions, AI, and infrastructure sectors. However, further analysis reveals that this altcoin rally was not evenly distributed, but rather dominated by a very small number of exceptionally performing assets. The top 10 gainers all exceeded 100%, with RAVE, Binance Life, and ORDI leading the pack with gains of 1596.2%, 307.2%, and 265.4% respectively. The remaining 20 tokens saw relatively moderate performance, mainly concentrated in the 40%-100% range, averaging around 65.6%. In other words, a few top performers drove the overall performance. Trading volume data also confirms this. Although overall trading volume has rebounded somewhat, funds haven't flowed evenly but are concentrated on a few tokens. In the past 24 hours, the average trading volume for these tokens was approximately $140 million, with ORDI, BIO, and BASED alone accounting for nearly 70% of the trading volume, demonstrating a strong concentration of funds. If we break it down further, the assets that have risen in this round can be roughly divided into two categories: One category consists of projects that have just reached new highs and are in a strong uptrend. Twelve projects have reached new highs within a month, with most breaking their all-time price records in the last two days. These tokens have good liquidity and high market attention, but have already accumulated a large amount of profit-taking, and their current prices have retreated by an average of about 29.5% from their peak. Once new funds weaken or sentiment shifts, a sharp correction is likely. The other category consists of deeply oversold rebound assets. Thirteen tokens have been away from their all-time highs for more than a year, with the longest span exceeding four years, and an average maximum drop of 95.4%. Because the actual circulating market capitalization of these projects has shrunk significantly, only a small amount of capital is needed to quickly drive up the price, with an average increase of 104.4% in the recent rebound. From a market capitalization perspective, most cryptocurrencies currently have a market capitalization concentrated in the range of $20 million to $80 million. These low-to-mid-market-cap projects, due to their smaller circulating supply, exhibit significantly stronger price elasticity under the same amount of capital inflow compared to high-market-cap projects, making them more prone to rapid price surges. Looking at the eight projects with market capitalizations exceeding $100 million, the average increase is approximately 340%, indicating that these projects had relatively low initial market capitalizations, allowing them to achieve such strong gains. However, as market capitalization is rapidly inflated, achieving similar increases will become significantly more difficult. Limited Rebound Potential, Altcoins Enter an Era of Differentiation Although altcoin prices and trading volumes have shown signs of recovery, given the overall insufficient liquidity in the crypto market and Bitcoin's continued dominance, the rebound potential may be relatively limited. CoinGlass's altcoin seasonal index shows a current index of 36, indicating that the overall market has not yet experienced a broad-based altcoin season, with most tokens underperforming Bitcoin and funds concentrated in a few strong performers. Meanwhile, CoinGlass's Fear & Greed Index, a barometer of crypto market sentiment, shows a current index of 22, placing it in the fear zone. This low level reflects cautious sentiment among market participants, indicating an overall oversold or pessimistic state. The fear zone has historically occurred approximately 30.68% of the time, a common characteristic during bear markets in crypto, suggesting that the current rebound is not yet based on a strong emotional resonance. More importantly, the current altcoin rally is still occurring during a Bitcoin-dominated phase. According to CoinGecko data, Bitcoin currently holds a 56.8% market share in the crypto market, while Ethereum holds 10.7%. Until Bitcoin's absolute market share declines significantly, altcoins remain more of a rotating asset rather than a leading trend. This round of altcoin gains may be driven by the following key factors: First, easing geopolitical risks. Easing geopolitical risks have provided significant macroeconomic support for this altcoin rebound. Recent signs of easing tensions between the US and Iran have significantly reduced market risk premiums, driving a collective recovery in global risk assets. As a high-beta asset, the crypto market is highly sensitive to changes in geopolitical sentiment. After risk aversion eased, investor risk appetite rebounded, and some profit-taking funds rotated from mainstream assets like Bitcoin to more volatile altcoins, amplifying the upward surge of some strong performers. Secondly, there was profit-taking rotation. Bitcoin's recent sustained strength, approaching $75,000, has yielded substantial returns for early investors. As Bitcoin entered a period of high-level consolidation and its upward momentum slowed, some profit-taking funds began to flow out, shifting to altcoins with smaller market capitalizations and greater potential for growth, seeking higher returns. In particular, many established altcoins were in severely oversold territory; any liquidity replenishment could trigger a rapid rebound, further amplified by the derivatives market, pushing prices even higher. However, it's important to note that the rebound in these assets relies primarily on sentiment recovery or external catalysts, rather than fundamental improvements. Thirdly, there was significant market manipulation by large investors. The violent surges in a few cryptocurrencies are attributed to the concentrated control of tokens by project teams or market makers. These assets have extremely low liquidity, making their prices easily manipulated; they are essentially more akin to liquidity games than free market pricing. For example: RAVE had over 90% of its supply concentrated in three wallets suspected to be controlled by a team or internal entities called Gnosis Safe. By inducing short sellers to build positions, the supply tightened, causing a short squeeze and leading to a price surge. Following this sharp rise, the market immediately saw large-scale selling pressure and whale dumping. SIREN was also observed to have experienced intense market manipulation after market makers regained control of over 93% of the tokens, resulting in a significant price increase. During the surge in Meme's Binance Life token, its controlling group continuously increased its holdings, at one point exceeding 22%. Thirdly, narrative and news-driven catalysts. Factors such as funding progress, sector rotation, and the driving effect of popular concepts like AI provide short-term momentum for price increases, but their sustainability and upside potential are limited, with the overall trend being more transaction-driven than trend-driven. It's important to note that among tens of millions of altcoins, only a very small number are currently capable of truly driving prices up. Bitwise's Chief Investment Officer, Matt Hougan, recently pointed out that the traditional altcoin season with widespread price increases may have ended, and the future will see a more differentiated and unconventional altcoin cycle. CryptoQuant analyst Darkfost also stated that with approximately 47 million cryptocurrencies currently issued globally, this massive quantity directly leads to severe liquidity dilution, making altcoins increasingly vulnerable. Overall, while a few strong altcoins amplify the market effect, most assets remain in a liquidity vacuum. Investors should remain vigilant and wary of profit-taking pressure, manipulation by major players, and sharp fluctuations caused by low liquidity.

data.title: Has the Year of Blockchain Arrived on Wall Street? From Regulatory Crackdown to Compliance and Coexistence
data.description:
One ​​bounce does not make a bull. The current price is still anchored within a structured range of Realized Price ($54.4k) and True Market Mean ($78.4k). Until it firmly establishes itself above the key resistance level of $78.4k, the market's return will remain tentative.

Crypto Market Summary

During this bi-weekly observation period, Bitcoin successfully broke out of the $62.8k–$72.6k stalemate, briefly touching the $74k mark. This breakout strategically signifies that the market has moved from a geopolitically induced "forced deleveraging" phase to an "initial stabilization period."

However, as leading asset management institutions, we must remain calm: "One bounce does not make a bull." Before firmly establishing itself above the key resistance level of $78.4k, the market's return remains tentative. Glassnode data shows that support on the spot market is slowly recovering, and after continuous outflows, the 7-day moving average of US spot ETFs has turned positive. Institutional buying interest around $65k has reaffirmed the role of ETFs as a defensive center on the spot market. Macroeconomic Focus and Key Areas of Concern 1. Supply Clearing, Confidence Uncertain The market is at a crossroads of "supply clearing and uncertainty." Subsequent data analysis will demonstrate that this transition is accompanied by a sharp shift of liquidity towards high-certainty assets. The "shakeout" signal in STH-SOPR: The current 7-day average of the Short-Term Holder Payout Profit Margin (STH-SOPR) is only 0.985. This means that recently entered investors are still in a loss-making position. "So What?" Dimension: The current profit supply ratio has only recovered to about 60%, which historically belongs to the "first wave exhaustion" level at the bottom of a cycle. Only when this ratio continues to stand above 75% can the true transition from bear market recovery to the main upward wave of a bull market be confirmed. Currently, we are in the painful period of conversion of token holders from "tenants" to "owners" (Conviction Pending), and the oscillation and shakeout are a necessary process to accumulate long-term confidence. 2. Wall Street's on-chain integration with AI-Web3 Public chain ecosystems are shifting from "narrative-driven" to "industry application-driven". This transformation is no longer a simple technological iteration, but a reconstruction of governance logic.

I. Market Overview

1. Mainstream MarketSituation

The total global cryptocurrency market capitalization is $2.49 trillion, up from $2.45 trillion last week, representing a week-on-week increase of approximately 1.63%. Bitcoin's market capitalization is $1.42 trillion, accounting for 57.02% of the total cryptocurrency market capitalization. Meanwhile, stablecoins have a market capitalization of $310.3 billion, representing 12.46% of the total cryptocurrency market capitalization. The cryptocurrency fear index is 13, indicating extreme fear.

2. RWA Market Size

RWA.xyz data shows that the value of distributed assets has reached $27.35 billion. This growth is no longer a simple "mapping," but rather an industrial transformation of traditional financial infrastructure through "Staked Liquidity" (MSL) systems, represented by Midas—solving the redemption queuing problem through a pre-allocated funding layer, thus realizing the upgrade of RWA from a laboratory model to a patch for large-scale financial applications. Data growth is clearly showing a trend of "violent equalization of liquidity premiums." This on-chain migration driven by real assets forces the regulatory framework to respond defensively. II. Hot Market News The public blockchain ecosystem is shifting from "narrative-driven" to "industry application-driven." This transformation is no longer a simple technological iteration, but a reconstruction of governance logic. 1. US Stocks on Blockchain and Financial Infrastructure Restructuring: A “Reduced-Dimensional Attack” on TradFi Nasdaq Tokenization Pilot: The SEC approved DTC to conduct a three-year pilot program for tokenization services, using a “Stock Transformation Gateway.” The most crucial breakthrough is that tokenized securities share the same order book with traditional settlement, achieving seamless “hot-swappable” operation of the traditional clearing chain. S&P 500 On-Chain Deployment: S&P 500 Authorizes Trade XYZ to Launch Official Perpetual Contracts on Hyperliquid, Supporting 50x Leverage and Directly Utilizing S&P DJI Institutional-Grade Raw Data. This "Institutional-Grade Data + Decentralized Execution" model represents a game-changer for the traditional 5x8 trading system in finance. Jupiter and Pre-IPO Trading: By listing tokenized equities such as OpenAI and Anthropic, Jupiter is releasing liquidity premiums from unlisted unicorns, representing a powerful equalization of liquidity in Silicon Valley's equity market. 2. The Convergence of AI and Web3: Capital's Occupation of Sovereignty Nous Research's Valuation Benchmark: Nous Research, the team behind Hermes Agent, completed a funding round with a token valuation of $1 billion, with investors including Paradigm. This signifies that crypto capital is no longer satisfied with simple speculation, but has begun to "occupy sovereignty" over top-level open-source AI frameworks. Mining Companies Transform into HPC: Mining companies such as Bitdeer and Riot leverage their grid resource advantages to transform into high-performance AI computing, achieving strategic synergy between energy assets and cutting-edge computing power. 3. Governance Transformation and VC Survival Rules Solana's "HDB Politics": Drawing on Singapore's governance logic, Solana is forcibly transforming "floating speculators (tenants)" into "asset holders (owners)" through airdrops (such as JUP, JTO) and staking mechanisms. "Only those with assets will protect the country"—this binding of interests is the underlying logic for combating centrifugal forces within the community. VC Paradigm Thinking: As Dragonfly stated, the industry is shifting from "heroism" to rewarding "stable and sustainable contributors." Venture capital firms must align with LPs' demand for certainty (stablecoins, perpetual contracts) rather than blindly betting on disorderly innovation. III. Macroeconomic and Regulatory Dynamics US regulation is undergoing a strategic shift from "encirclement" to "rule-based survival." Recent White House actions indicate that crypto regulation is no longer just a financial issue, but also a geopolitical game for maintaining the digital sovereignty of the US dollar. 1. In-depth analysis of the Clarity Act: Policy rifts and sovereign competition. This act is a nuclear-level tool for reshaping the landscape of the "digital dollar," and is currently in a critical period of clearing the final technical hurdles: The core game and the challenge from the Association of Bankers (ABA): A compromise has been reached on the controversy over whether stablecoins should pay yields. Although a White House report claims this will not shake the foundation of bank deposits, the ABA strongly opposes this, arguing that the report has serious flaws. This open rift between institutions reflects the deep-seated fear of "liquidity disintermediation" within the traditional banking industry. Key Obstacles Cleared: Negotiations have made substantial progress in sensitive areas such as DeFi illicit finance protection measures and the Democratic Party's demand for "restrictions on senior officials (such as Trump) working in the industry." 2. Breaking the Impasse on Administrative Exemptions and State Reserves: SEC Administrative Exemptions: The SEC has explicitly guided that self-custodied wallet interfaces that do not actively solicit or guide routing choices will be exempt from brokerage registration. This provides a legal loophole for the front end of decentralized protocols to survive. North Carolina Bill 327: This bill has passed its first reading and proposes to authorize the allocation of 10% of public funds to Bitcoin. Its importance lies not only in the allocation ratio but also in its stringent state-level auditing and multi-signature cold storage standards, formally establishing Bitcoin's legal status as a "state-level strategic asset." 3. Global Geopolitics and Geofinancial Integration: The direct talks between the US and Iran scheduled for April 16 in Islamabad have provided a buffer period for market risk aversion. At the same time, the European Central Bank (ECB) insists that tokenization must be pegged to central bank currency and maintain strict interoperability, reflecting Europe's defensive regulatory stance against the fragmentation of the financial system.

data.title: The focus of competition in stablecoins is shifting.
data.description:
The discussion surrounding stablecoins has long focused on one question:Who will issue the next dominant stablecoin?

However, Circle, a stablecoin issuer, is diminishing the significance of this question itself through its recent actions in South Korea.

In his latest statement, Circle CEO Jeremy Allaire explicitly stated that there are currently no plans to launch a stablecoin for the Korean won.
